So, I am on item 48, I think page 75, about installing the familiar. So, it had not actually occurred to me until I read it. It probably occurred to many of you and is known to you. Hey, by the way, we're going to show, yeah. Well, let me do that first. Can you cue it? Okay. We've just a little video here related to installing the familiar. You know, once in a while, something comes along that changes the way we live. A device so simple and intuitive, using it feels almost familiar. Introducing the 2015 IKEA Catro. It's not a digital book or an e-book. It's a book book. The first thing to note is no cables, not even a power cord. The 2015 IKEA catalog comes fully charged and the battery life is eternal. The interface is 7.5 by 8 inches, but can expand to 15 by 8 inches. The navigation is based on tactile touch technology that you can actually feel. It comes pre-installed via 328 high definition pages of inspiring and furnishing ideas. To start browsing, simply touch and drag. Right to left to move forwards. Left to right to move backwards. Notice something else? That's right. No lag. Each crystal clear page loads instantaneously, no matter how fast you scroll. If you want to get a quick overview, just hold it in the palm of your hand and using just your thumb, speed us to content. If you find something you want to save for later, you can simply bookmark it. And even if you close the application, you can easily find a bookmark again. Amazing. What about multiple users? For that, we've introduced a simple color coding system to avoid confusion. If you want to share a particularly inspiring item, you literally share it. Another special feature is password protection, which is voice activated. Excuse me, that's mine. At IKEA, we feel that technology, this life enhancing, should be in the hands of everyone. So the 2015 IKEA catalog is free. You can download one from your mailbox, the one you open on the key. If it's not there, try to refresh the next day. Or you can upload yourself to the IKEA store and find one there. Experience the power of a bookbook. I believe that is an actual ad that they are running, not just for fun. So installing the familiar. For some reason, it did not occur to me until I read recently, but it is glaringly obvious. Well, so I'm now quoting Steve Jobs that we decided to, here it is, this is my start. We decided to abandon archaic typing commands, a blinking cursor, control panels, and replace it with something every office worker was already familiar with, a desktop. It was at Apple that we transformed the Mac screen into a virtual desktop with file folders, a clipboard, scissors, and paste, and even a trash can at its side. The trash can even was made to bulge until it was empty. You simply had to point and click, not type in a command. We built it as a metaphor so we could leverage the experience people already had. That's really cool. I mean, that's really cool because people are always trying to understand this in the context of something they already understand. It tastes like chicken, which by the way, anytime somebody tells you, it tastes like chicken. First of all, it probably doesn't. Second of all, it's some noxious thing you shouldn't be eating. And third of all, why not eat chicken? It's easy to get chicken. In Arizona, when I lived in Arizona, they served people rattlesnake as an appetizer, like deep fried, of course, if you deep fried cardboard, but deep fried rattlesnake. And people will always, well, what does it taste like? Oh, it tastes just like chicken. I'm thinking, I have chicken tenders. A rattlesnake is a poisonous animal full of venom. I mean, I don't know that it. But that's how people relate to things, right? This is like that, but different or better or whatever. And so what Jobs did was brilliant when you think about it. And so it gets us back to now on page 76 that we looked at it once before in a different context, the points of powerful analogy. When you are explaining something to someone, then analogy is very, very powerful. One of the most successful lead generation ads that, actually two of them, that Darren Garman and I developed for his business, which is investing in apartment buildings and shopping centers and commercial properties in Iowa. So other than the Darren Garman personality driven ads, which by far is still the most successful, frustrated Iowa prison guard discovers, frightened Iowa banker discovers, those ads still outpoll anything else. A close second came in, a safe CD you can drive past and show your friends. And we made the whole analogy case that certain kinds of real estate investing is like having a CD, except you can actually drive up in the park a lot and you can show people you own it. The other ad that was very effective was be a landlord to the United States Postal Service or Walmart. And of course they do both lease and rent space. What sounds more secure than being the landlord of Walmart? Because everybody thinks they ain't going nowhere. Wrong, by the way, but that's neither here nor there. So, analogy, well everybody thought that about Radio Shack. I mean, everybody thinks that about everything. That whatever is around for a while is going to be around forever. Everybody thinks that. That by the way, not on a list, but you could play to that as a hijack because everybody does believe it. If it's like been there probably a decade or more, most people think it's going to be there forever. And history would tell us that is not true. So this is your analogy list. And analogy and metaphor are both very, very powerful. They're helpful in making people understand and get quickly comfortable with something that is new to them. And the familiar, we underestimate how uncomfortable people are with the unfamiliar. I talked about it a little bit in the context of us not thinking we're scary to other people. So whatever is not familiar, it's like going to a seminar. The general public doesn't go to seminars. So that's an uncomfortable, unfamiliar idea to them. You pretty much know exactly what it's going to be like. They don't know what it's going to be like. In professional practices, we do the some version of the what to expect on your initial appointment in painstaking, elementary, simplicity, and completeness. Number one, you will arrive at the office and you will be greeted by a friendly, smiling person. Number two, you're going to have to fill out some forms and we're going to make it as painless as possible. Then only take 13 minutes and you won't be asked anything that you don't know. So what to expect at your first appointment? What's going to happen? Because they want to know what's going to happen in any setting where they don't already know what's going to happen. People bring people to meetings and hotels. A lot of people go to a hotel five times in their whole life. They don't know what that's like. They don't know. They don't know how to dress, where to park. So when you can make the unfamiliar, familiar for people, that's really helpful. I'll show you a familiarity direct mail trick. So this is, and my clients have six different ones. I'm just going to show you one. So this is a wrapper. So it could go around any sales letter, any direct mail piece bound in the middle and we do use it with different ones at different times. Anyone given lists may see this four times during the course of a year if they're mailed 24 times. So this uses Life Magazine and it's a wonderful life movie. We're going to a demo of 60 years of age and up. They all got Life Magazine. They know what it is. They all know what it's a wonderful life is with Jimmy Stewart, etc. That movie, most of you probably try not to watch. Maybe once is enough. In some households, it's an annual, everybody watches It's a Wonderful Life every year for Christmas. My own preference, my Christmas movie is I Watch Bad Santa. The Billy Bob Thornton movie, if you haven't seen it. That's now my Christmas classic. Right? Carter leaves the room, everybody leaves the room, I Watch Bad Santa. That's just, to me, that's it. But yeah. So this goes as a wrap inside. It's got a little kind of Norman Rockwell little picnic thing and a little Woody with the side. And the inside back is a comic strip like they all grew up with looking in a newspaper. So we've taken the pitch and wrapped it in the familiar and triggering nostalgia. So he just was showing me, what card did you just buy? What is it? Yeah, gorgeous by the way. And he said correctly, so the classic car market is divided into three things. There's investors, there's people who do it like you trade stocks or trade real estate or you know whatever. There's fixer uppers like in real estate. And then there's just the consumer guy who has a car. And he almost always is buying the car he had as a kid, his dad had when he was a kid or the car he wanted when he was a kid that he couldn't afford. That's what's getting bought, right? And they're paid premium prices because that's not the point. And because I drive them, people are constantly doing what he just did. They want to show me the pictures in their phone of their car and they want to tell me their car story, which is why I've told all the financial advisors, financial advisor, you got to go get yourself one. I don't care if you don't want one, right? And if you're not going to drive it much, get one that barely runs but looks good. Park it in front of your office, right? 25% of the people coming through, you're going to have instant rapport with people because it's going to remind them of what they had, what dad had, what granddad had, what they wanted and now a whole different conversation is going to occur and their trepidation about doing business with you as a financial advisor is going to go down. This is really designed to do the same thing, right? It's designed to carry the water of the marketing message and make the marketing message less scary because we have surrounded it with things that are familiar to them and that they have kind of warm, pleasant feelings about. Let's skip 49 for the sake of conversation. Let's go to, you're going to run that other video in just a sec. Yeah, water. Let's go to encouraged assumption. Just as everybody has a belief system about just about everybody and everything, everybody's really quick to assume things. So there was an experiment done. Let me see if I can get, let me see if I can find the exact notes. They had, they had a guy go walk up and down busy street, stop where people were waiting to cross the street carrying a big red umbrella on a perfectly sunny day. Here's what happens. Only half of the people around him started to check weather forecasts on their smartphones. Other people ask him, hey, is it going to rain today? When quizzed by researchers who then snagged them as he walked away, the majority of these people had watched weather forecasts, heard them on the radio or checked them by computer before leaving their house. And now a short time later, they encounter a stranger with a red umbrella and they assume it's going to rain and now try and find out about that. So it says two things about people. One, they don't have a lot of confidence and faith in themselves and in whatever decision they already made and whatever information they already gathered. It doesn't take much to unsettle that and cause them to question it. Most of all, they're not very discerning. So they're going to ask a stranger they don't know who is walking around on a sunny day with a red umbrella. Now you could assume the guy's a nutball, but that's not the assumption they made. The assumption they made is, it's very important, he must know something I don't know. And that's a very useful assumption to trigger. And all it took was a red umbrella. So if we think consumers got smarter, so let me give you history and let me give you current. So here's history. For his museum, for which traffic was a problem, P.T. Barnum, some of you will know the brick story, he sent a guy out with an arm load full of bricks and had the guy walk slowly around several blocks all the way back to the entrance to the museum, every so often, carefully putting a brick in position on the ground, standing back, kind of looking at it, making sure he had it in the right place, then walking some more and then putting down another brick and putting down another brick. Couple people started to follow him, few people started to follow him, more people started to follow him, and they followed him all the way back around to the entrance of the museum into which he walked, and they followed him, paying to get in as they followed him into the museum. Things in the museum that interested them and they stayed and looked at Barnum's stuff in the museum. By the guy's fourth circuit around, he was swarmed with lots of people asking him questions, to which he just shrugged it off and they followed him. By the end of the first day, the guy with the bricks had drawn over a thousand people into the museum. Three days later, the cops ordered it stopped because the crowds were interfering with traffic. Now, fast forward to June 10, 2016. As a prank, two California teenagers put a pair of eyeglasses on the floor of the San Francisco Museum of Art. If you can see it, it's a little picture, but here's the picture. They just put a pair of glasses down there, and here's some that went, taken pictures of it, on the ground. Says something about art, too, right? Fair enough, visitors crowded around the stranded glasses, mistaking them for an avant-garde exhibit. The teenagers had been amused that some of the museum's exhibitors were considered art in the first place. The 16-year-old said, this seemed pretty easy. So, he and his friend placed Kevin's eyewear on the floor. Within minutes, admiring visitors were gathering around the glasses and taking photos of them. No, they haven't got any smarter. No! Barnum then, 16-year-old kid with a Vietnamese name, so he's smarter than the rest of us, doing it now, right? Doesn't matter. Instant assumption. We're in an art museum. There's other weird shit. There's a trash can lid over there, so this has got to be art, right? So people make assumptions all the time. The only question is whether you want to benefit from it or not, whether you want to align with it or not, whether you want to manipulate it or not. So the one I like the best, which we have tightly edited, some of you have seen the whole episode. I think we're only going to use seven or eight minutes. Three and a half, okay? So this is from, so you're going to see the whole thing, and the whole series is really worth seeing if you're about to binge watch something on a rainy day. This is from Penn and Teller Showtime Show, which is called Bullshit. And it's about water. And so this starts at a real high-end restaurant, I believe in Beverly Hills, if I'm not mistaken. I would people pay such a premium for bottled water. Is it all in their minds? To find out, we took over a very trendy California restaurant. We hid our cameras on the balcony to watch. Instead of a wine steward, we created the world's first water steward. We printed our own elegant water menus with phony imported waters, costing as much as $7 per bottle. What was the actual source of these chic waters? A garden hose on the restaurant patio. Oh, I love this fucking show. Good evening, folks. Hi, how are you doing? Welcome. My name is Tim, and I will be your water steward tonight. Our water steward gives our first lucky couple our special water list. Like a wine list. What do you want to try? You want to try this? Sounds French. Yeah. I'll get the Ladoo Roubignet. The Ladoo Roubignet? Yeah. Oh, fantastic. It's French for tap water. And for you, sir, would you like to try one, maybe compare notes? Yeah, do the other one. Sure, I'll do the Mount Fuji. The Mount Fuji? Ah, two excellent choices. Yeah, sure. Okay. Yeah, which is clean. It's taste. It has a flavor to it. How would you compare it to tap water? Oh, yeah, it's definitely better than tap water. It doesn't have an active taste to it. It's got a flavor that it almost feels like beverage other than water without sugar or any additives. Ladoo Roubignet passed the taste test. How about Mount Fuji? Which of course came from the same funky oz. It's right from the top of Mount Fuji. It's a very pure water. It's a natural diuretic and an antitoxin. It does feel glacier-like. Glacier oriented. Glacier oriented. Yeah, this is good. Yeah. I've taken the liberty of bringing you a bottle of our Agua de Culo. Okay. It's 650 per bottle. It's got a very fresh taste to it. It's crisper. It has a crisp taste. I'm not a kid. Surely this couple wasn't representative. Let's see how some other people rated our premium hose water. It seems smoother than tap water. Better than LA County water. We're pretty sure the hose added that glacial taste that was so widely praised. Consumers are offered water with names conveying pure and pristine water sources. Ever hear of Alaskan Falls water? Water must come from the crystal clear glacier waters of our 49th state, right? Sorry folks. Alaskan flows from this Liquibox Corporation building in Worthington, Ohio. Does the brand Yosemite conjure up visions of the cool pristine waters rushing through California's high Sierras? Well then, the marketers have done their jobs. The source of Yosemite bottled water is actually 400 miles away in northeast Los Angeles. How about Everest water? Could our piddly show possibly afford to send a camera crew to Nepal? Not necessary. Everest comes from the industrial section of Corpus Christi, Texas. In fact, if you read the fine print on the fucking label, they even admit the water comes from a municipal source. That is tap water, brothers and sisters of the cult of the bottle. Now think of everything, think of all the familiar that they did that lends itself to assumption, right? I mean, the menu, the language, at least one of them is French, the guy, the water comes over with the ice bucket that stands by the side of the table. I mean, it's perfect, right? And the guy conned him with the one because he lied to him. But if you took that out, if you had never said this comes from the top of the mountain in Nepal, it wouldn't have made any difference. The whole bit would have gone on regardless, right? Because of these two things combined, familiar and assumption. Those two things combined drive it all. I think that if you carefully analyze it, here's all the other hacks. So number 12, symbols and symbolism. Number 14, showmanship. Number 15, optics. Number 21, transfer of authority, credibility and believability because of the restaurant alone. You're in an expensive restaurant, right? 33 false choice. That's all one big false choice. But he even foisted a third bottle on them on the first couple on top of the two they had ordered and nobody rebelled. Ego hooks, of course, and it went showing off, right? And preexisting beliefs. We kind of believe there's good water somewhere. And most people believe generally bottled water is better for them than other water. In fact, I just saw in the news yesterday or today that bottled water sales now in the U.S. have surpassed all soft drinks added together, which is why all the soft drink companies have been busily getting in this business. So bottled water now is the number one selling beverage and it's all what they just showed you. It's all right out of the municipal water supply in Detroit with a fancy label on it. Well, I didn't say Flint. With a fancy label on it, that's all it is, right? But it works for this reason. So now when you think about this, think about physical choreography, environment, you know, so I used to fight with the chiropractors, tooth and nail in the 1980s because, and you said it this morning, somebody said it or one of the Q&As about people's expectations when they come in an office. So chiropractors, then they're still pretty bad at it, but it was a big thing. They're like famously, at the same time they're annoyed at not being viewed as real doctors. They're famous for not dressing like doctors. They're coming to work in the Hawaiian patterned shirt and khaki slacks and sandals, you know. Worse, the staff is all mismatched dressed, too. Well, first of all, in the 1980s, there were all these doctor shows on here and T.C. was still around, doctor killed their general hospital was a big deal. What you see on all those shows is you see a doctor and you see a doctor staff and they're all in white coats and they all got their names on them. They're in a Hawaiian shirt. I mean, unless they're in some mash, maybe, I think the doctors were in Hawaiian shirts. But in practices, people have a sense of what that's supposed to look like and when it doesn't look like that, they are unreassured. I don't think that's a word, but it should be. That's like, what's the one Trump uses all the time that's not bigly? This is a bigly idea. That's his, I check just to be sure. It's not a word. But it's going to be by the time he gets done with everybody. So I would desperately try and get these Kyros to dress like doctors and we empirically proved if you put that guy in a white coat and you hung a stethoscope around his neck, now Kyropracers don't use stethoscopes. There's no reason to use a stethoscope. I don't give a shit. Hang it around your neck. It's a symbol. And you put the staff in matching uniform coats, not polo shirts with a logo on them. They like doing that too. So let's, in our doctor office, let's dress everybody like the sandwich artist at Subway. This will be reassuring to people coming here for their healthcare. So they dress them up like they work at Subway or they work at a car repair place. There's also a movement, you'll see them in bowling shirts. They're all in matching, hang outside your pants, bowling shirts. I'm going, guys, we empirically proved. Took a guy who didn't fix anything else. Just fixed staff uniforms, his uniform, hung a stethoscope around his neck. His closing rate on prepays went up. This stuff matters, right? And it matters in everything. So you've got to figure out what do they expect, what's reassuring to them, what's unsettling to them, what will encourage an assumption that is useful to us. And if you're going to violate it, have a reason for violating it and a context to violate it in. So it's not that you can't, but you've got to be really cognizant of what you're doing. The apparel that a speaker wears on stage. I've proven it to myself again and again and again. Suit, outsells, sport coat and slacks. Tie, no tie, tie outsells. There's certain colors for guys that work and certain colors for guys that don't work. Even with the young hip internet geek audience, so like I did Yonix thing one year, I'm the only guy dressed. All the other speakers are dressed like internet geeks, working at home in mom's basement. So they're like you. So they got a hoodie on or they got a t-shirt on with a picture of Albert Einstein upside down and some cute quote. And they got droopy pants or cargo shorts or something and no socks and some of them throw a blazer on over top of this concoction. And they go out on stage and they attempt to sell stuff. And they think that's okay because their audience looks like that. And it helps. It's better than if.
